flashcards.back: Any waste generated during diagnosis, treatment, or immunization of human beings/animals, or in research activities related thereto.
The systematic handling, storage, collection, transport, treatment, and disposal of waste produced in healthcare facilities.

flashcards.front: What are the main sources of biomedical waste?
flashcards.back: Hospitals, nursing homes, clinics, diagnostic labs, blood banks, research laboratories, veterinary institutions, and mortuaries.

flashcards.front: List 5 categories of biomedical waste under BMW Rules 2016
flashcards.back: 1) Infectious waste 2) Pathological waste 3) Sharps 4) Pharmaceutical waste 5) Cytotoxic waste
Waste containing pathogens in sufficient concentration to cause disease (cultures, swabs, bandages, gloves, contaminated disposables).
Human tissues, organs, body parts, fetuses, animal carcasses, and blood/blood products.
Needles, syringes, scalpels, blades, broken glass, broken ampoules, and any items with sharp edges that can cause cuts/punctures.
Expired drugs, antibiotics, cytotoxic drugs, vaccines, and discarded containers of medicinal products.

flashcards.front: What does yellow category of BMW include?
flashcards.back: Human anatomical waste, soiled waste, infectious waste, incineration ash, chemical waste.
Contaminated waste (recyclable) - tubes, bottles, IV sets, catheters, gloves, masks, head covers, foot covers.

flashcards.front: What is the blue/white translucent category?
flashcards.back: Sharps - needles, syringes, blades, broken glass, metal implants, broken/used slides.

flashcards.front: What is the black category for?
flashcards.back: General waste - paper, cardboard, plastics without contamination, food waste, garden waste.

flashcards.front: What is segregation of biomedical waste?
flashcards.back: The process of separating different categories of waste at the point of generation to ensure safe handling and proper treatment.
Reducing the generation of waste at source through proper planning, inventory control, and using alternative methods.
A thermal treatment method using saturated steam under pressure to sterilize infectious waste (121°C at 15 psi for 30 min).
